result:
At the edge of the city, where the last apartment blocks give way to fields, there is a narrow footbridge over a railway line. I like to stand there just before sunrise. The rails hold the night a little longer than the grass does; they shine with a cold, blue patience while sparrows begin arguing in the hedges. Far away, a signal changes from red to green, and the small light seems less like an instruction than a promise.

Nothing important happens there, which is why the place feels honest. No monument explains what to feel. No shop window suggests what to want. There is only wind, gravel, weeds pushing through concrete, and the occasional train carrying strangers toward meetings, hospitals, schools, reconciliations, errands they will forget by evening. For a few seconds each passing carriage frames a face, a book, a hand around a paper cup, then everything is gone.

I think people need such places: unofficial thresholds where the mind can loosen its knots. We spend so much time being named by tasks that we forget the quieter self that simply notices. A bridge at dawn can return that self, not dramatically, but like a borrowed coin found in a coat pocket.

When the sun finally lifts over the warehouses, the rails flare gold. Traffic wakes behind me. I turn back toward the city, carrying no revelation, only the useful feeling that the day has not yet decided what it will be. That, sometimes, is more than enough.

The rails hold the night a little longer than the grass does; they shine with a cold, blue patience while sparrows begin arguing in the hedges. Far away, a signal changes from red to green, and the small light seems less like an instruction than a promise.

Nothing important happens there, which is why the place feels honest. No monument explains what to feel. No shop window suggests what to want. There is only wind, gravel, weeds pushing through concrete, and the occasional train carrying strangers toward meetings, hospitals, schools, reconciliations, errands they will forget by evening. For a few seconds each passing carriage frames a face, a book, a hand around a paper cup, then everything is gone.

I think people need such places: unofficial thresholds where the mind can loosen its knots. We spend so much time being named by tasks that we forget the quieter self that simply notices. A bridge at dawn can return that self, not dramatically, but like a borrowed coin found in a coat pocket.

When the sun finally lifts over the warehouses, the rails flare gold. Traffic wakes behind me. I turn back toward the city, carrying no revelation, only the useful feeling that the day has not yet decided what it will be. That, sometimes, is more than enough.
